title: Select relevant 3D lights per mesh on GLES3 and Mobile renderers
body:
This PR changes the behavior of the `RendererSceneCull::_scene_cull` function to select the most relevant 3D lights per mesh in the Compatibility and Mobile renderers.  It is a fix for [Issue 107070](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/107070).

Previously, if the scene had many more lights in range than could be rendered per-mesh (so > 8 for Mobile, or > `Max Lights per Object` for Compatibility), Godot would simply pick the first N lights and assign them to the mesh.  Even increasing the limits (on Compatibility) failed to illuminate the scene as you would expect.  I am sure most users who need complex 3D lighting can use the Forward+ renderer, but I am hoping to get a decent approximation running on the more limited renderers (I ran into the issue trying to fake GI in a procedural building with lots of lights).

Here is the behavior of 4.4.1 in a scene with 256 meshes and 256 omni-lights.  For testing purposes, the range of each light was extended so that all meshes were in range of all lights.  The result looked the same on both Mobile and Compatibility (with limits set to 256 lights per scene, 8 per-mesh to match Mobile):
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/84221bad-476d-4bcf-9e86-fe5cf483c16c)

With the code modifications in this PR, it now looks like this:
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/56710ad3-3227-456e-8334-1da10a872958)

The original code ran through each mesh, and then each light relative to each mesh.  The new code follows the same pattern but now assigns a score to each light, relative to each mesh, factoring in the distance from the light's AABB center to the mesh's AABB center, the light range, and the light energy.

The code then keeps track of the top N lights, by keeping the top N scores in a SortArray, which uses a heap internally to keep the scores sorted enough to find the worst score directly (it is always in slot [0]).  If a new light is found to score better than the worst in the collection, slot 0 is replaced with the new light and the heap is updated.  

In theory this is the fastest way to run through *all* M lights while keeping the top N, however many spatial hashing schemes could accelerate this by not having to run through all M lights for every mesh.  So there is room to optimize further if this turns out to be too slow for extreme cases, however 256 3D lights in a scene might be excessive for mobile and web targets.  [8^)

- _Bugsquad edit,_ fixes https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/107070
author: jon1solution

linked_issues.title: Irrelevant 3D lights are chosen per mesh in Mobile and Compatibility renderers

issue_comments.body:
> Here is the behavior of 4.4.1 in a scene with 256 meshes and 256 omni-lights.

Can you upload this test project somewhere? This will make it easier to test the PR.
Thank you for the feedback! I forgot to mention, in the original MRP, if you change the Grid Size export var up to 16, you should have the 256 meshes and lights test case displayed.
Looks like a nice first contribution. Great talking it all through in the rendering meeting. Few tidbits and this should be a nice improvement.

> Looks like a nice first contribution. Great talking it all through in the rendering meeting. Few tidbits and this should be a nice improvement.

Thank you!  I think it is ready for another look.

I've tested this PR again on the MRP included in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/110085, and noticed that it *reverses* the issue in both Mobile and Compatibility. It no longer occurs near the origin, but it starts occurring far from the origin (where it looked correct previously).

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9ce9316e-5426-4205-a51c-c828118907f0

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/71f49ac0-9104-4b7e-b844-e64a7d467571

> I've tested this PR again on the MRP included in #110085, and noticed that it _reverses_ the issue in both Mobile and Compatibility. It no longer occurs near the origin, but it starts occurring far from the origin (where it looked correct previously).

Thanks for testing!  Could you please zip that tiny test project and drop it here?  I should have some time early next week to look at this.

> Thanks for testing! Could you please zip that tiny test project and drop it here? I should have some time early next week to look at this.

[lights.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22044861/lights.zip) (it's linked under "Minimal reproduction project" in the linked issue)

> [lights.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22044861/lights.zip) (it's linked under "Minimal reproduction project" in the linked issue)

Thanks.  It seems the lights aren't getting culled when they are outside the view frustum prior to being linked to mesh instances (quite possibly by design, since this linking does not take camera position into account?).  So in the test case, all 9 lights attempt to link, the 8 of the 9 (basically) closest to the center of the mesh are kept...same problem as in the original, only it's a different light that loses the toss.

review_comments.body:
Use a space instead of a tab between `//` and the first character of the comment (this applies to other comments in this PR too):

```suggestion
						// Clear any existing light instances for this mesh and return the max count per-mesh, and total (per-scene).
```

Finally got around to doing a bunch of testing @jon1solution. Played around a bit with the test projects. First there does seem to be a bug. `heapify.adjust_heap` nicely inserts the new entry but it doesn't actually end up sorting the `LocalVector`, so the more lights it evaluates, the more likely you get the wrong lights. Actually testing more, even after `make_heap` is called the sorting can be off: So it might even be a bug in the sort logic itself, or we're making an assumption here on what 'make_heap' and 'adjust_heap' supposedly do. Testing in OpenGL with only 2 lights, it masks the issue, but testing in the mobile renderer with 8 lights you do end up with the wrong lights. Second, and I can't put my finger on it completely, `geom->lights` seems to always contain all lights that are in range of the mesh regardless of whether the light itself is being culled. Or maybe there is some other criteria for what gets added here. As a result, this code path is skipped with the previous results reused until `FLAG_GEOM_LIGHTING_DIRTY` gets set. I can pass several lights and have them out of range behind the camera before the light pairing is updated and even then it seems to still process all lights. Third, we're sorting by distance to the mesh. This solution is perfect for your use case (in which the second point is also mute), with many objects potentially being effected by many lights, sorting the lights and having those closest to the object win is perfect. But the other sample from @Calinou 's comment, here all 3 points mentioned effect the outcome. The sorting would mean that only the lights closest to the center of the mesh would be applied, regardless of the camera position. This use case would work better if we'd prioritise lights based on distance to the camera, but that would break your use case. I don't think the distance fade is taken into account here either, you'd expect it to adjust the content of `geom->lights` but it didn't seem to do so for me. -- Anyway, to make a long story short. My recommendation is to figure out what is going on with the sorting and fix that issue. Then this PR solves your use case, which IMHO is all we can fix with this optimisation. For the issues with the test project @Calinou was referring too, I think that requires an entirely different approach. We'd need to look into why `geom->lights` includes lights that should be culled because they shouldn't effect anything on screen. Neither fixes the issue where there are more then 8 lights (2 for GLES3) that effect one mesh and where we'd like them sorted based on distance to camera instead of distance to mesh. I think those requirements are mutually exclusive. Personally I believe distance wins out because you can solve the other use case by simply breaking your mesh up into smaller meshes.

label_notes: Substantial GLES3/Mobile renderer fix: most-relevant-lights-per-mesh selection for RendererSceneCull. jon1solution (first-time contributor, +181/-45, 9f) provided test project and completed 12 review iterations.
